Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'm 28 and working part-time while doing this, so I'd squeeze in studying whenever I could—early mornings before work, late nights after everything else was done. I tried to block out chunks on weekends, maybe 4-5 hours, but it rarely went according to plan. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes rather than just reading through materials linearly.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening in my life outside school. Some weeks I'd be consistent, logging in every other day, but then work would pick up or I'd have a rough week mentally and everything would shift to deadline-driven cramming. I tried to establish regularity, but with limited resources and competing demands, consistency was a luxury I couldn't always afford.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because my anxiety would spike thinking I'd forgotten something. Honestly, boredom with the routine of life sometimes pushed me to log in too—like, at least doing coursework felt productive even when I wasn't feeling motivated.
Q4: There were brief periods, maybe a couple weeks at a time, where logging in became automatic—like part of my morning coffee routine. It happened when the course content was actually engaging or when I was in a good headspace. But those windows were fragile. One stressful week at work or a bout of high anxiety and the habit would break completely.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly the most useful for me because I'm such a visual learner. I watched over 200 minutes of them, often pausing to sketch out concepts or take screenshots. The discussion posts were helpful too when people actually engaged meaningfully—seeing different perspectives helped things click. Anything with diagrams or visual explanations stuck with me way better than walls of text.
Q6: I tended to ignore or skim the dense reading materials, especially PDFs that were just text-heavy. My eyes would glaze over and nothing would stick. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions beyond what was required—only did about 5 of those. Partly because I felt awkward being older than most students, partly because I just didn't have the mental bandwidth to socialize academically.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to the assignments or exams, or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. Visual content, interactive elements, anything that broke the monotony—those felt valuable. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade, my motivation to engage dropped fast, especially when I was already stressed.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Part of me knew they could help, but when you're juggling five online courses with limited time and energy, optional means 'skip this.' Required activities had urgency attached to them. Optional ones just added to my anxiety because I'd feel guilty not doing them but couldn't justify the time when mandatory stuff was piling up.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them offline because my internet isn't always reliable and I couldn't afford to depend on being connected. I'd print out slides, make handwritten notes, use free YouTube videos to supplement concepts. My physical notebook probably has more evidence of my learning than the LMS ever would.
Q10: There were many times I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then spend hours afterward working through the concepts on paper, making visual maps, or explaining things to myself out loud. I'd also study with downloaded materials on the bus to work. None of that shows up anywhere in the system, but that's where a lot of my actual learning happened.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture or reading once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never touch the LMS version again. My task completion looks okay at 79%, but the reality is I engaged with materials way more than my login frequency suggests. I just couldn't afford to stream videos multiple times or keep refreshing pages.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My stress was through the roof, anxiety spiking constantly, and I was just bored with the material—not because it was easy, but because everything felt monotonous and overwhelming at the same time. Work demands increased, I was exhausted, and logging into the LMS felt like another chore rather than something meaningful.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or confused, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities—work, bills, life stuff—meant the LMS often got the tired, depleted version of me. Confusion with course material sometimes made me avoid logging in altogether because facing what I didn't understand felt too overwhelming.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. My body was there, my clicks were registered, but my brain had checked out. High anxiety does that—you go through the motions without being present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety around them was paralyzing sometimes—like, I knew I needed to do them, but starting felt impossible. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was less tired or stressed, but tomorrow always came with its own problems. My assignment completion at 66% reflects that struggle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd have low activity for days, then a burst of frantic engagement right before something was due. It's not healthy and I know it, but without that external pressure, my low motivation made it hard to self-start. The LMS probably shows these spikes clearly—quiet periods followed by intense activity.
Q17: Feedback affected me a lot emotionally but not always productively. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback—even constructive—would send my anxiety spiraling. I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. When I did get helpful feedback, I tried to apply it, but the emotional toll made engagement complicated.
Q18: The structure was both helpful and frustrating. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to work toward, which I needed. But with five online courses, the structure sometimes felt suffocating—too many deadlines clustered together, not enough flexibility for someone balancing work and limited resources. I appreciated the organization but wished for more breathing room.
Q19: Visual materials and clearly organized modules made engagement easier. What made it harder was when instructions were vague or when I had to dig through multiple pages to find what I needed. Also, required peer interactions felt forced and awkward. The mindfulness intervention they offered was actually helpful for my stress, but it didn't fix the underlying issues with workload and anxiety.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my worst weeks—logging in repeatedly because of anxiety, clicking around trying to figure out what's due, watching videos without absorbing them, but being too overwhelmed or confused to actually complete submissions. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it just means struggling visibly.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My LMS engagement was technically 'low,' but I passed with decent exam scores because I did a lot of learning offline. If someone is efficient with their time, downloads materials, studies independently, they might not show much LMS activity but still perform. The platform only captures one slice of the learning process.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I was actually learning or just anxiously refreshing pages. They miss offline study, emotional state, life circumstances. They're a skeleton of engagement, not the full picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material—not just completing tasks but understanding them, finding them relevant, wanting to learn more. It's a mental and emotional state, not just behavioral. For me, true engagement happened in quiet moments with my notebook, not necessarily when I was clicking through the LMS.
Q24: The LMS can't show my resilience—how I kept coming back despite high anxiety, low resources, and constant stress. It can't show the hours I spent studying offline, the mental battles I fought to submit assignments, or how the mindfulness exercises helped me cope. It sees a student with low engagement, but it misses someone who fought hard just to pass.
